So he actually puts in quotes, a few things like he's gone into the into every single reader's mind, pulled out the internal dialogue and put it on the page, which, which can never be correct. I mean, that's, that's presuming a lot, as opposed to allowing people to come to the conclusion on their own. So when you paint a picture in a certain way about where somebody is now, what the future could look like and what that bridge could be moving somebody from where they are now to their future desired, you know, their future desired self. So, you know, I just feel that people need to come to their conclusion on their own when a picture is forming in their head and when certain things are set on the page. And it's the most effective way of doing that is to paint this picture where people come to their conclusion on their own. The two plus two idea, whereas if you give them, you know, when you give them to you allow them to figure out the other piece themselves. So you give them to and you give them to, but you don't give them for you don't tell them how they need to think you don't tell them what the what the aha moment is or what the insight needs to be. You know, that's, that's, that's just telling as opposed to showing. So that's my ramble on the my negatives of the bridge email. And it's interesting, you know, the two plus two instead of four idea, I want to mention one thing I'm going to do a steep a speed dating version of my feedback when I go through it just line by line. But before I do that with two plus two, it's amazing to me how often we get emails back and someone reveals an aha that they had like, oh, and you know, the thing that you wrote is changed xyz. It was so amazing. And then they share what they took from what we wrote. And it has no relationship whatsoever to what we wrote, or it has no, like it was not at all what we meant. But they they had a meaningful epiphany. So we created the vehicle, and then they filled, you know, we gave them two plus two, but they came up with their version of four was it was it was informed by their context what they needed. And they had a they had a valuable experience. But it was not at all what we were talking about, which sometimes is puzzling to me, right? Like, I think that's, and oftentimes it's the opposite of what we meant. So if you try to constrain too much what you're if you're trying to speed spoon feed the reader, too much, you can backfire you need to let them fill in their own blank blank space in their own context. All right, so this is my speed dating version of the feedback. So right in the beginning, after orienting himself says we've covered a lot, we started with a problem. Why your business likely isn't growing as fast as it should. That is the that's just that's a terrible problem. Like that's the wording for that. I read that I go, my business likely isn't growing as fast as it should. You know, I can't help but yawn when I read that sentence. That needs to be the problem needs to be hard hitting, and it needs to be visceral, and it needs to be emotional. So when you say we started with the problem, that problem better make me feel like I'm taking a deep breath like, shit, that is the problem. Or just make it hit hard. We started with the problem. Your business isn't growing as fast as it should. That hits harder, right? But you don't like the rest of it is just that mealy mouth kind of not wanting to say what you're actually saying. So just say it. And I would dimensionalize it with some emotion. Like when I think about the problems, I think about the problems and how the problem feels, because there's the problem, the thing they can point to. But that's not the real problem. The problem is how they feel about the thing that they point to. So if the problem is you're lying awake at night, and you don't know how you're going to make cash flow, or how you're going to make payroll because of cash flow that month, that's the problem you can point to the external problem. But it's the fact that you feel like a failure. That's the real problem. That's the internal thing that feels miserable. So when you say we started with the problem, make it a problem, make it the thing that keeps them awake at night, and the way that thing makes them feel, and then you're going to get their attention. Yeah, I mean, I see what he's doing here, which, you know, we all have to do this to get to the other side of it, which is you get 200 ideas in your head and you've got to get 200 ideas onto the page. And what we do is experts, this is the curse of knowledge. The curse of knowledge is that when Brian is writing a sentence or a series of sentences because he's an expert and he knows what he's talking about, he's filling in the context as he goes naturally, but the reader doesn't see it. The reader's not actually inside his head. So when he's talking about, you know, to creation mode, optimizing the entire business for happy, successful customers, evolving ourselves in the process in order to clearly see what needs to happen next, the brain chemistry is totally different once you give yourself permission. And it's like, what? But Brian is having, he is just naturally filling in all of the pieces where we're feeling this. And that's one of the hardest things about being, about having expertise and writing for someone who doesn't have the same level of expertise is that it's very easy to fill in and not know that you're filling things in so that when the reader is reading, he or she isn't able to fill in what's in your mind, it's a very different experience. I'm going to skip email five for now. You covered email five. Something I think is very interesting. Email six, the frame, the visuals in email six, I think belong in the bridge email. And slightly, I don't think it's ironic. It's just interesting, but it's a bridge. The metaphor that he's using is our half bridges. So bridges in a bridge email, I guess that's redundant in a really wonderful way. So here's the imagery that Brian has introduced, which I think it's outstanding. You've got two mountains separated by a huge valley, person on the left who represents the customer pot of gold is on the right. And what does the person do? They hear that whatever they're supposed to do Facebook advertising. So they build the bridge halfway there. And when they get to halfway, the bridge halfway there, they find out, oh, no, it's Instagram stories and they build another half bridge and they keep doing this and they never actually get to the pot of gold. Instead, they build whatever, 10, 15, 20 half bridges. I think this isn't the bridge email. I think if he took email number six and gave people that opportunity to nod her heads, yes, go, oh my God, that's me. Yes, I do that or to to at least introduce the idea and then later introduce the how they so I think I think the bridge email can become it keep the orienting in the beginning like all of that part, but then introduce this metaphor of building half bridges. Because once you introduce that metaphor right before you reveal the link to move forward is when you can explain to them to the audience that what you're going to show them how to do is to actually build the bridge one at a time. Now is now you've created the metaphor and now you don't actually have to you don't have to do as much work to get the click forward. The right people will continue the journey because now they have a metaphor. There's an analogy and we learn there's actually there's a presentation that's called like analogy as the core of cognition and it's this there is there is an underlying idea in cognitive science that we understand the world around us through analogy, through metaphor. So when you give somebody this and it's a it's a masterful analogy when you give somebody or metaphor I tell them the difference is probably something should be on my shortlist the thing to figure out but when you give somebody this visual framework of like hey this is what you want here's you in the mountain here's the pot of gold on the other mountain it seems insurmountable there's this massive gulf between what you where you are and what you want. So you do what any normal person would do as you start building bridges to the other side except here's the problem you don't actually build the bridge you start to build the bridge and then you get distracted and think and you can explain to them why whatever you can explain this is this is natural you get distracted to wonder to be fearful maybe this isn't the right bridge that's an interesting thing to introduce because you've identified and you're Brian later mentions he identified 72 bridges or something but to to create this framework for the person to think about it it's classic what you did with Frank and Matt where they then self-identify like son of a bitch I keep doing these half bridges right I tried Facebook advertising but it never really worked I never really built that bridge or I tried whatever it is chat bots it didn't quite work now they've entered in the narrative you've created they see themselves as the person on the mountain and they want the pot of gold and they see what they're doing to sabotage that and because you've made that the frame you now that the this is a perfect example of where your offer becomes the logical next step because you have put them inside a frame where they are trying to get what they want but they're doing this thing that prevents them from getting it and you're going to show them how to do the thing that actually gets it instead to build the bridge from one side to the other and get the benefit of that before they build the next bridge now they want to move forward now they understand everything that after that now fits into a mental construct for them where it's like okay they're going to show me how to build these these bridges to get there to get to that pot of gold that that's a masterful making that the bridge we're identifying the first thing that you positively need to do now not the only thing we're just going to help identify the first thing so if you're sitting here there today and you know you've got shiny object syndrome if you're sitting there today and you know that you have a warehouse full of half built bridges you want to finish something and make sure you're finishing the right bridge that's what I'd love to help you with click on the link below to book your session now only a limited number of slots available okay here's the trick and I don't mean trick in a negative context the trick in here is you want to finish something and make sure you're finishing the right bridge this is the problem that you're actually introducing for them that only you can solve and that's what makes somebody need to sign up for this session because and you need you need to structure this differently the like all of it has to be structured around this idea that they there you've
